BOOK ONE

CHAPTER I

The first volume opens upon a wide and windswept plain where the grasses bent
in long silver waves beneath a sky the colour of old pewter, and a single rider
made his slow way toward the smudge of smoke that marked the distant settlement.
He had been many days upon the road and his horse was tired, and he too was
tired in a way that sleep could not mend, carrying as he did the weight of news.

CHAPTER II

In the settlement they took him in and fed him and asked no questions until he
had eaten, as was the custom of that country, and only afterward did the elders
gather in the long house to hear what he had ridden so far and so hard to tell.
He spoke plainly and without ornament, as a man does who has rehearsed his words
across a hundred miles of empty country, and when he finished no one spoke.

BOOK TWO

CHAPTER I

The second volume begins a full year later and many leagues to the south, where
the rains came warm and constant and the rivers ran brown and swollen to the
sea. Here the same news had travelled by other roads and other mouths, growing
and changing as such things do, until what arrived bore only a passing likeness
to the careful words the rider had spoken in the cold long house of the north.

CHAPTER II

And so it was that two versions of a single truth came at last to stand opposed,
each believed entirely by those who held it, each the seed of all that followed.
The chronicler who set these things down so long afterward could no longer say
with certainty which had been the nearer to the facts, and confessed as much,
and left it to the reader to weigh the matter and to judge of it as seemed best.
